title: EXPOSÉ IX SOME COMPUTATIONS OF K-GROUPS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ IX SOME COMPUTATIONS OF K-GROUPS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ IX
SOME COMPUTATIONS OF K-GROUPSby P. BERTHELOT

This exposé does not correspond to any oral exposé, and will not be used in the rest of the seminar. It contains a certain number of computations of K-groups, for various fibrations: vector bundles, projective bundles, torsors under a linear group, etc... Throughout the exposé, the base scheme will be assumed noetherian, and the notation \(K_\bullet(X)\) will denote the Grothendieck group of coherent sheaves on a scheme \(X\). Recall that if \(X\) is noetherian, the homomorphism from the K of the category of coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules to the K of the category of pseudo-coherent complexes on \(X\) is an isomorphism (IV 2.4).

The results presented here are the analogue of those obtained for the Chow ring, which can be found in Grothendieck's exposés in the Séminaire Chevalley 1958. The question also arises of determining to what extent statements of the same type can be obtained for the graded object associated to K. for the filtration defined by the dimension of the support (cf. X). The answer does not seem to be known, even for Proposition 1.1 (homotopy exact sequence), which is one of the most frequently used properties in the computations that follow.
1. Vector bundles

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1. (Homotopy exact sequence).

Let \(X\) be a noetherian scheme, \(X'\) a closed subscheme of \(X\), \(U\) the open complement of \(X'\) in \(X\), \(i\) the immersion of \(X'\) into \(X\), and \(j\) the immersion of \(U\) into \(X\). Then the sequence:

\[K_\bullet(X')\xrightarrow{i_*}K_\bullet(X)\xrightarrow{j^*}K_\bullet(U)\longrightarrow0\]

is exact.

If \(F'\) is a coherent \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-Module, \(i_*(F')\) is a \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module whose support is contained in \(X'\); consequently its restriction to \(U\) is zero, and \(j^*\circ i_*(\operatorname{cl}(F'))=0\), whence, the elements of the form \(\operatorname{cl}(F')\) generating \(K_\bullet(X')\), \(j^*\circ i_*=0\). From this we deduce a homomorphism
\[\rho:K_\bullet(X)/\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X')\longrightarrow K_\bullet(U).\]

We shall define a homomorphism inverse to \(\rho\). Let \(G\) be a coherent \(\mathcal O_U\)-Module. Since \(X\) is noetherian, there exists a coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(F\) whose restriction to \(U\) is isomorphic to \(G\). If \(F'\) is a second coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module whose restriction to \(U\) is isomorphic to \(G\), then we have in \(K_\bullet(X)\):
\[\operatorname{cl}(F)-\operatorname{cl}(F')\in\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X').\]
To see this, we regard \(G\) as a subsheaf of \(j^*(F\oplus F')\) by means of the diagonal map; \(G\) then extends to a subsheaf \(F''\) of \(F\oplus F'\), and we are reduced to showing that
\[\operatorname{cl}(F)-\operatorname{cl}(F'')\in\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X'),\]
and the analogue for \(F'\). Let then \(H\) and \(K\) be the kernel and cokernel of the composite homomorphism:
\[F''\longrightarrow F\oplus F'\longrightarrow F\ ;\]

this homomorphism reduces to the identity of \(G\) on \(U\), and consequently \(H\) and \(K\) have support contained in \(X'\). If \(I\) is the ideal of \(X'\) in \(X\), it follows that there exist integers \(m\) and \(n\) such that \(I^mH=0\), \(I^nK=0\) (EGA I 9.3.4). We can therefore break up \(H\) and \(K\), by dévissage, into extensions of Modules
annihilated by \(I\), hence of \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-Modules, and we have:
\[\operatorname{cl}(H)\in\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X'),\qquad \operatorname{cl}(K)\in\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X'),\]

whence the result. We therefore define a function on the category of coherent \(\mathcal O_U\)-Modules with values in \(K_\bullet(X)/\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X')\) by associating to every coherent \(\mathcal O_U\)-Module the class of one of its extensions over \(X\).
This function is additive. Indeed, let:
\[0\longrightarrow G'\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow G''\longrightarrow0\]

be an exact sequence of coherent \(\mathcal O_U\)-Modules. Let \(F\) be an extension of \(G\) over \(X\); then there exists a sub-Module \(F'\) of \(F\) extending \(G'\), and \(F/F'\) extends \(G''\). It therefore factors through \(K_\bullet(U)\), which defines the desired homomorphism \(K_\bullet(U)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)/\operatorname{Im}K_\bullet(X')\). One checks immediately that it is inverse to \(\rho\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2.

Let \(X\), \(S\) be two noetherian schemes, and \(f\) a flat morphism from \(X\) to \(S\). We give ourselves a family of elements \((\xi_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(K'(X)\), and we consider the homomorphism:

\[\varphi:(K_\bullet(S))^{(I)}\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

defined by:

\[\varphi((x_i)_{i\in I})=\sum_{i\in I}\xi_i\,f^*(x_i),\]

where \(f^*\) is the "inverse image" homomorphism from \(K_\bullet(S)\) to \(K_\bullet(X)\), defined according to IV 2.12, and where \(K_\bullet(X)\) is regarded as a \(K'(X)\)-module by IV 2.10.

We suppose that for every point \(s\in S\), the images of the \(\xi_i\) in \(K'(X_s)\) under the homomorphism

\[K'(X)\longrightarrow K'(X_s)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\]

generate \(K_\bullet(X_s)\) as an abelian group. Then \(\varphi\) is surjective.

Let \(S'\) be a closed subscheme of \(S\), and \(U\) the open complement of \(S'\) in \(S\). We denote by \(X_U\) the restriction of \(X\) above \(U\), and by \(X_{S'}\) the restriction of \(X\) above \(S'\). Finally, we again denote by \(\xi_i\) the images of the \(\xi_i\) in \(K'(X_{S'})\) and \(K'(X_U)\), and we denote by:
\[\varphi_{S'}:K_\bullet(S')^{(I)}\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_{S'}),\qquad \varphi_U:K_\bullet(U)^{(I)}\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_U)\]
the homomorphisms defined in the same way as \(\varphi\).
We consider the diagram

\[(1.2.1)\qquad \begin{array}{ccccccc} K_\bullet(S')^{(I)}&\xrightarrow{i_*}&K_\bullet(S)^{(I)}&\xrightarrow{j^*}&K_\bullet(U)^{(I)}&\longrightarrow&0\\ \downarrow{\varphi_{S'}}&&\downarrow{\varphi}&&\downarrow{\varphi_U}\\ K_\bullet(X_{S'})&\xrightarrow{u_*}&K_\bullet(X)&\xrightarrow{v^*}&K_\bullet(X_U)&\longrightarrow&0 \end{array}\]

where \(i\) and \(j\) denote the immersions of \(S'\) and \(U\) into \(S\), and \(u\) and \(v\) the immersions of \(X_{S'}\) and \(X_U\) into \(X\). Let us show that if \(\varphi_{S'}\) and \(\varphi_U\) are surjective, then the same holds for \(\varphi\).
The two horizontal rows of the diagram are exact by 1.1. We then see that in order to show that \(\varphi\) is surjective, it suffices to show that the two squares are commutative.
Let \((x'_i)_{i\in I}\in K_\bullet(S')^{(I)}\); we have:

\[\begin{aligned} \varphi\circ i_*((x'_i))&=\varphi((i_*x'_i))=\sum_i\xi_i\,f^*(i_*x'_i),\\ u_*\circ\varphi_{S'}((x'_i))&=u_*\left(\sum_i\xi_i\,f_{S'}^*(x'_i)\right)=\sum_i\xi_i\,u_*(f_{S'}^*(x'_i)), \end{aligned}\]
the last equality following from the projection formula IV 2.11.1.2.
The equality \(\varphi\circ i_*=u_*\circ\varphi_{S'}\) therefore follows from the equality \(f^*\circ i_*=u_*\circ f_{S'}^*\), which holds by IV 3.1.1, since \(i\) is proper and \(f\) flat.
Let \((x_i)_{i\in I}\in K_\bullet(S)^{(I)}\); we have:
\[\varphi_U\circ j^*((x_i))=\sum_i\xi_i\,f_U^*\circ j^*(x_i)\ ;\]
\[v^*\circ\varphi((x_i))=v^*\left(\sum_i\xi_i\,f^*(x_i)\right)=\sum_i\xi_i\,v^*\circ f^*(x_i)=\sum_i\xi_i\,f_U^*\circ j^*(x_i),\]
the middle equality following from IV 2.12.2.

This result allows us to prove 1.2 by noetherian induction on the set of closed subschemes of \(S\). If we suppose that for every closed subscheme \(S'\) of \(S\) distinct from \(S\), \(\varphi_{S'}\) is surjective, then the preceding argument shows that we may reduce to proving 1.2 for an irreducible affine base scheme, after replacing \(S\) if necessary by an irreducible affine open subset. Moreover, the diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} K_\bullet(S_{\mathrm{red}})^{(I)}&\longrightarrow&K_\bullet(S)^{(I)}\\ \downarrow{\varphi_{S_{\mathrm{red}}}}&&\downarrow{\varphi}\\ K_\bullet(X_{S_{\mathrm{red}}})&\longrightarrow&K_\bullet(X) \end{array} \ ,\]
where \(K_\bullet(X_{S_{\mathrm{red}}})\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\) is surjective by 1.1, shows that we may also suppose \(S\) reduced.

We therefore place ourselves over an integral affine base scheme. We set \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and denote by \(K\) the field of fractions of \(A\); if \(s\) is the generic point of \(S\), then \(\mathcal O_{S,s}=K\).

Let \(y\in K_\bullet(X)\). By hypothesis, \(K_\bullet(X_s)\) is generated by the images of the \(\xi_i\); if \(y_s\) is the image of \(y\) in \(K_\bullet(X_s)\), we can find integers \(n_i\) such that:
\[y_s=\sum_i\xi_i\,n_i\ .\]
Moreover, we have \(K=\varinjlim_f A_f\), where \(f\) runs through the set of elements of \(A\). Consequently, \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)=\varprojlim_f\operatorname{Spec}(A_f)\), and:
\[X_s=X\mathbin{\times_S}\operatorname{Spec}(K)=\varprojlim_f X\mathbin{\times_S}\operatorname{Spec}(A_f),\]
by EGA IV 8.2.3 and 8.2.5. Since the schemes under consideration are noetherian, and the transition morphisms affine and flat, we have by IV 3.2.3:
\[K_\bullet(X_s)=\varinjlim_f K_\bullet(X\mathbin{\times_S}\operatorname{Spec}(A_f))\ .\]

Since the "inverse image" homomorphisms on the K. are linear with respect to the K' \(IV (2.12.2)\), there exists a nonempty open subset \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A_f)\) of \(S\) such that in \(K_\bullet(X_U)\) we have:
\[y_U=\sum_i\xi_i\,n_i\ .\]
Let \(S'\) be a closed subscheme of \(S\) with support \(S-U\). Then \(\varphi_{S'}\) is surjective by the induction hypothesis, and consequently the diagram (1.2.1) shows that:
\[y-\sum_i\xi_i\,n_i\in\operatorname{Im}(\varphi)\ ,\]
hence, since \(n_i=f^*(n_i)\), \(\varphi\) is surjective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a flat morphism of noetherian schemes. We suppose that for every point \(s\) of \(S\), the homomorphism:

\[K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\]

is surjective. Then the homomorphism deduced from \(f^*\):

\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

is surjective.
We take for the family \((\xi_i)_{i\in I}\) the family of all the elements of \(K'(X)\). The hypotheses of 1.2 are then satisfied, and consequently the homomorphism:
\[\varphi:(K_\bullet(S))^{(K'(X))}\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]
is surjective. By the definition of \(\varphi\), this amounts to saying that the homomorphism:
\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]
is surjective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a flat morphism of noetherian schemes. We suppose that for every point \(s\) of \(S\), the canonical homomorphism \(\mathbb Z\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\) is an isomorphism. Then the homomorphism:

\[f^*:K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

is surjective. If moreover \(f\) possesses a section \(g\) of finite tor-dimension, then \(f^*\) is an isomorphism.
We take for the family \((\xi_i)_{i\in I}\) the family reduced to the single element \(1\in K'(X)\); we then have \(\varphi=f^*\), and, the conditions of 1.2 being fulfilled, \(f^*\) is surjective.

If \(f\) admits a section \(g\) of finite tor-dimension, we can define a homomorphism \(g^*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(S)\) (IV 2.12). Since we have \(g^*\circ f^*=(f\circ g)^*=\operatorname{Id}\) by IV 2.12.1.1, \(f^*\) is injective, hence is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.5.

For \(g\) to be of finite tor-dimension, it is necessary and sufficient that the fibres of \(X\) be regular at the points of \(g(S)\), i.e. that \(f\) be regular at the points of \(g(S)\) (or again smooth at these points if we suppose \(f\)

of finite presentation).

These properties being local on \(X\) and \(S\), we may suppose them affine; let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\). We then have:

\[\operatorname{tor.dim}_B(A)=\operatorname{dim.proj}_B(A)=\sup_{s\in S}\operatorname{dim.proj}_{B\otimes_A k(s)}(k(s))\]

by EGA IV 12.3.2, where the finite presentation hypothesis on \(B\) may be replaced by noetherian hypotheses. Now \(k(s)\) is isomorphic to the residue field of \(\mathcal O_{X_s,g(s)}\), and by EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 17.2.5 we have:

\[\operatorname{dim.proj}_{B\otimes_A k(s)}(k(s))=\operatorname{dim.proj}_{\mathcal O_{X_s,g(s)}}(k(s))\ .\]

It then follows from EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 17.2.11 that the latter is finite if and only if \(\mathcal O_{X_s,g(s)}\) is of finite cohomological dimension, that is to say regular.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of finite type, \(X\) the vector bundle associated to \(E\), and \(f\) the structural morphism of \(X\). Then \(f^*:K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\) is an isomorphism.

Since \(f\) is smooth, and admits a section, namely the zero section, to prove 1.6 it suffices to show that for every \(s\in S\), \(K_\bullet(X_s)\simeq\mathbb Z\) (1.4). We are thus reduced to the case where the base scheme is a field.

In this case, \(E\) is a direct sum of submodules of rank 1, which allows us to decompose \(f\) into a sequence of fibrations of rank 1, and hence to reduce to the case where \(X\) is of rank 1, say \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(k[T])\). The proposition then follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7.

Let \(A\) be a principal ideal ring, \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Then the canonical homomorphism:

\[\mathbb Z\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

is an isomorphism.

Since \(X\) is regular, the homomorphism \(K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\) is an isomorphism. Hence \(K_\bullet(X)\) is generated by the classes of the locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type, i.e. by the classes of the projective \(A\)-modules of finite type. Since \(A\) is a principal ideal ring, every projective module of finite type is free, whence the surjectivity. The injectivity comes from the fact that \(K'(X)\) is \(\mathbb Z\)-augmented.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.8.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(r\), \(X\) the vector bundle associated to \(E\), \(f\) the structural morphism of \(X\), and \(g\) the zero section of \(X\). Then the composite homomorphism:

\[K_\bullet(S)\xrightarrow{g_*}K_\bullet(X)\xrightarrow{g^*}K_\bullet(S)\]

(where \(g^*\) is defined according to IV 2.12.1 and 1.5) is multiplication by the element \(\lambda_{-1}(E)\) of \(K'(S)\), where \(E\) denotes the class of \(E\) in \(K'(S)\), and where we set \(\lambda_{-1}(E)=\sum_{i=0}^r(-1)^i\lambda^i(E)\).
This statement is the analogue, for the K., of VII 2.7.

Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module, with class \(F\) in \(K_\bullet(S)\). Then \(g_*(F)\) is the class in \(K_\bullet(X)\) of the complex \(\mathbf Rg_*(F)=g_*(F)\), hence of \(F\) regarded as an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module by means of \(g\). To compute \(g^*(g_*(F))\), we must therefore take the class of \(\mathbf Lg^*(F)=F\overset{\mathbf L}{\otimes}_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_S\).
For this, we consider the complex:

\[0\longrightarrow \Lambda^r(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}_{\mathcal O_S}}\mathcal S(E)\longrightarrow \Lambda^{r-1}(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}_{\mathcal O_S}}\mathcal S(E)\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}_{\mathcal O_S}}\mathcal S(E)\longrightarrow\mathcal S(E)\longrightarrow0\]

where \(\mathcal S(E)\) is the symmetric algebra of \(E\). It is a resolution of \(\mathcal O_S\) over \(\mathcal S(E)\) (VI 1.11). The associated complex on \(X\), denoted \(L_\bullet\), has as its \(k\)-th term \(\Lambda^k(f^*(E))\) and is therefore a resolution of \(\mathcal O_S\) by locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type; we therefore have \(L_\bullet\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}F=\mathcal O_S\overset{\mathbf L}{\otimes}_{\mathcal O_X}F\). Consequently:

\[\begin{aligned} g^*(g_*(F))&=\operatorname{cl}(L_\bullet\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}F)=\sum_{i=0}^r(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}(L_i\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}F)\\ &=\sum_{i=0}^r(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}(\Lambda^i(E)\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}F)\\ &=F\,\lambda_{-1}(E). \end{aligned}\]
The proposition follows from this by additivity.
2. Principal bundles under split tori

2.1. Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and \(T\) a torus over \(S\). We suppose \(T\) split; we therefore have an isomorphism \(T\simeq D_S(M)\), where \(M\) is a free abelian group of rank \(r\), or again \(T\simeq\mathbb G_{m\,S}^r\). Let \(P\) be a torsor under \(T\). Then \(P\) is affine over \(S\), defined by a \(\mathcal O_S\)-Algebra \(A\) graded of type \(M\); if we set \(A=\bigoplus_{m\in M}A_m\), then \(A_m\) is an invertible \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module for every \(m\), and, for \(m,m'\in M\), the homomorphism \(A_m\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}A_{m'}\longrightarrow A_{m+m'}\) defined by the multiplication of \(A\) is an isomorphism (SGA 3 VIII 4.1). We therefore define a multiplicative homomorphism \(\eta:M\longrightarrow K'(S)\) by associating to \(m\in M\) the class of \(A_m\) in \(K'(S)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.

With the notations and hypotheses of 2.1, let \(f:P\longrightarrow S\) be the structural morphism of \(P\). Then the homomorphism:

\[f^*:K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\]

is surjective, and its kernel is the subgroup of \(K_\bullet(S)\) generated by the subgroups \((\eta(m)-1)K_\bullet(S)\) for \(m\in M\), or, what amounts to the same thing, for \(m\) running through a system of generators of \(M\).
We first suppose \(r=1\), i.e. \(T=\mathbb G_{m\,S}\). Then \(M=\mathbb Z\), and we have for every \(m\in M\):
\[A_m\simeq A_1^{\otimes m}.\]

Consequently, if we set \(A_1=L\), we have \(A=\bigoplus_{n\in\mathbb Z}L^{\otimes n}\), which shows that \(P\) is identified with the complement of the zero section in the bundle \(X=\mathbb V(L)\). Regarding \(S\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\) by the zero section, we have by 1.1 the exact sequence:
\[K_\bullet(S)\xrightarrow{g_*}K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\longrightarrow0\]

where \(g:S\longrightarrow X\) is the zero section of \(X\). Moreover, \(f^*\) factors as \(K_\bullet(S)\xrightarrow{f'^*}K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\) where \(f'\) is the structural morphism of \(X\). By 1.6, \(f'^*\) is an isomorphism, inverse to \(g^*\). We therefore have the exact sequence:
\[K_\bullet(S)\xrightarrow{g^*\circ g_*}K_\bullet(S)\xrightarrow{f^*}K_\bullet(P)\longrightarrow0,\]
from which we obtain by 1.8:
\[K_\bullet(P)\simeq K_\bullet(S)/\lambda_{-1}(L)K_\bullet(S)=K_\bullet(S)/(1-L)K_\bullet(S)\]
where \(L\) is the class in \(K'(S)\) of \(L\). Since for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), \(L^n-1\) is a multiple of \(1-L\), this proves 2.2 in the case where \(P\) is of rank 1.

We continue the proof by induction on the rank \(r\) of \(P\). Suppose the result proved for torsors under split tori of rank \(<r\). We then write \(M=M_1\oplus M_2\), with \(M_1\simeq\mathbb Z^{r-1}\) and \(M_2\simeq\mathbb Z\); we set \(A'=\bigoplus_{m\in M_1}A_m\) and \(A''=\bigoplus_{m\in M_2}A_m\), so that \(A=A'\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}A''\). Let \(P_1=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\); then \(P_1\) is a torsor under \(D_S(M_1)\) and \(P\) is a torsor over \(P_1\), with group \(\mathbb G_{m\,P_1}\) and with algebra the inverse image of \(A''\) on \(P_1\). By what precedes, \(K_\bullet(P)\) is the quotient of \(K_\bullet(P_1)\) by the subgroup generated by the \((\eta(m_2)-1)K_\bullet(P_1)\) for \(m_2\in M_2\). By the induction hypothesis, \(K_\bullet(P_1)\) is the quotient of \(K_\bullet(S)\) by the subgroup generated by the \((\eta(m_1)-1)K_\bullet(S)\) for \(m_1\in M_1\). Taking into account the relation
\[\eta(m_1+m_2)-1=\eta(m_1)\eta(m_2)-1=\eta(m_1)(\eta(m_2)-1)+\eta(m_1)-1,\]

\(K_\bullet(P)\) is therefore the quotient of \(K_\bullet(S)\) by the subgroup generated by the \((\eta(m)-1)K_\bullet(S)\) for \(m\in M\), or again for \(m\) running through a system of generators of \(M\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3.

With the notations and hypotheses of 2.1, suppose that \(S\) is regular; then we have a canonical isomorphism:

\[K'(P)\simeq K'(S)/((\eta(m)-1)_{m\in M})\]

where \(((\eta(m)-1)_{m\in M})\) is the ideal of \(K'(S)\) generated by the \(\eta(m)-1\) for \(m\) running through a system of generators of \(M\).

If \(S\) is regular, the canonical homomorphism \(K'(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(S)\) is an isomorphism (IV 2.5). Similarly, \(P\) then being regular, \(K'(P)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\) is an isomorphism. The corollary therefore follows immediately from 2.2.
3. Projective bundles and flag bundles

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(n\), \(\pi=(p_1,\ldots,p_k)\) a sequence of integers such that \(\sum_i p_i=n\), and \(X=\mathbb D_\pi(E)\) the scheme of flags of type \(\pi\) of \(E\). Then the canonical homomorphism:

\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

is an isomorphism.

We take up again the notations of VI 4.5. We thus denote by \(L_i\) the canonical quotients of the inverse image of \(E\) on \(X\), by \(F_i\) the kernel of \(L_i\longrightarrow L_{i-1}\) (\(i=1,\ldots,k\)), and by \(\lambda_i^{(j)}\) the class in \(K'(X)\) of \(\Lambda^j(F_i)\). Then the \(\lambda_i^{(j)}\) generate \(K'(X)\) as a \(K'(S)\)-algebra (VI 4.6). Similarly, the images of the \(\lambda_i^{(j)}\) in \(K'(X_s)\) generate \(K'(X_s)\) as an algebra over \(K'(k(s))=\mathbb Z\) for every point \(s\) of \(S\). Since \(X_s\) is a flag scheme over a field, it is a regular scheme, so the homomorphism \(K'(X_s)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\) is an isomorphism, and therefore the homomorphism \(K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X_s)\) is surjective. By 1.3, this implies that the homomorphism:
\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]
is surjective.
To show the injectivity, we may first reduce to the case of the complete flag bundle \(\mathbb D(E)\). Indeed, suppose that
\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(\mathbb D(E))\longrightarrow K_\bullet(\mathbb D(E))\]
is injective. Since \(K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(\mathbb D(E))\) factors through \(K_\bullet(X)\), this
homomorphism factors as:
\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\otimes_{K'(X)}K'(\mathbb D)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\otimes_{K'(X)}K'(\mathbb D)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(\mathbb D)\]
and the first homomorphism is injective. Since, by VI 4.3, 4.6 and 4.7, \(K'(\mathbb D)\) is free over \(K'(X)\), this implies the injectivity of:
\[K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X).\]

So let \(X=\mathbb D(E)\). We proceed by induction on the rank of \(E\). The case \(n=1\) being trivial, suppose the property proved for \(n-1\). We then introduce \(P=\mathbb P(E)\), and the kernel \(F\) of the canonical homomorphism \(p^*(E)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(1)\), where \(p\) is the structural morphism of \(P\). Then \(X\) is identified with the flag scheme of \(F\) over \(P\), and we see by the induction hypothesis that it suffices to prove 3.1 for \(P\). Taking VI 1.1 into account, the result to be proved can therefore be stated as follows:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(n\), \(X=\mathbb P(E)\) the associated projective bundle, \(f\) the structural morphism of \(X\), and \(\xi\) the class in \(K'(X)\) of the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(1)\). Then the homomorphism:

\[\varphi:(K_\bullet(S))^n\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\]

defined by:

\[\varphi((x_i))=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\xi^{-i}f^*(x_i)\]

is an isomorphism.

We have already seen that this homomorphism is surjective, since the \(\xi^i\), \(i=0,\ldots,-n+1\), form a basis of \(K'(X)\) over \(K'(S)\); to see that it is injective, we use the homomorphism \(f_*\), which is defined since \(f\) is projective.
We then have, by IV (2.12.4):
\[f_*\left(\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\xi^{-i}f^*(x_i)\right)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}x_i f_*(\xi^{-i})=x_0,\]

the last equality following from the fact that for \(-n+1\leq i<0\), we have \(f_*(\xi^i)=0\) (VI 2.8). Suppose that \(\varphi((x_i))=0\). Then what precedes shows that \(x_0=0\); applying the same argument to the elements \(\xi^i\varphi((x_i))\) for \(i=1,\ldots,n-1\), we deduce by induction that the \(x_i\) are all zero, whence the injectivity.

3.3. Let \(S\) still be a noetherian scheme, and \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(n\). We denote by \(V=\mathbb V(E)\) the vector bundle (contravariant in \(E\)!) associated to \(E\) (SGA 3 I 4.6), regarded as a group scheme over \(S\). Let \(P\) be a torsor under \(V\); we propose to compute \(K_\bullet(P)\).
Let us recall how one can embed \(P\) in a projective bundle over \(S\). The torsors under \(V\) correspond to the extensions \(F\) of \(E\) by \(\mathcal O_S\). Indeed, if we have an extension:
\[(3.3.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S\xrightarrow{s}F\xrightarrow{u}E\longrightarrow0,\]

we deduce from it a torsor under \(V\) by considering the homomorphism \(\mathbb V(F)\longrightarrow\mathbb V(\mathcal O_S)\) defined by \(s\), the section of \(\mathbb V(\mathcal O_S)=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S[T])\) defined by \(T=1\), and by taking \(P=\mathbb V(F)\times_{\mathbb V(\mathcal O_S)}S\), the operations of \(V\) on \(P\) being induced by those of \(V\) on \(\mathbb V(F)\).

In the other direction, let \(P\) be a torsor under \(V\). We denote by \(\underline P\) the sheaf of germs of homomorphisms from \(S\) to \(P\); it is a principal homogeneous sheaf under the sheaf of germs of homomorphisms from \(S\) to \(V\), which is identified with the dual \(\check E\) of \(E\). From this we then deduce in the classical way an extension of \(\mathcal O_S\) by \(\check E\), whence by passing to duals the sequence (3.3.1).

So let \(P\) be a torsor under \(V\), corresponding to an extension \(F\) of \(E\) by \(\mathcal O_S\). The algebra \(A(P)\) of \(P\) over \(\mathcal O_S\) is \(S(F)\otimes_{S(\mathcal O_S)}\mathcal O_S\), that is \(S(F)/(s-1)S(F)\), which is identified with the ring of homogeneous fractions of degree 0 \(S(F)_{(s)}\). Consequently, \(P\) is identified with the open subset \(D_+(s)\) of \(\mathbb P(F)\). The complement of \(P\) in \(\mathbb P(F)\) is then \(\operatorname{Proj}(S(F)/s.S(F))\), which is identified by (3.3.1) with \(\mathbb P(E)\). We therefore have the immersions:
\[\begin{array}{ccccc}\mathbb P(E)&\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow&\mathbb P(F)&\longleftarrow\joinrel\lhook&P\\&\searrow&\downarrow&\swarrow\\&&S&&\end{array}\]
where \(\mathbb P(E)\) is closed in \(\mathbb P(F)\), and \(P\) open and complementary to \(\mathbb P(E)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(n\), \(V=\mathbb V(E)\) the associated vector bundle, \(P\) a torsor under \(V\), and \(f\) the structural morphism of \(P\). Then the canonical homomorphism:

\[f^*:K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\]

is an isomorphism.
With the identifications of 3.3, we have the exact sequence:
\[(3.4.1)\qquad K_\bullet(\mathbb P(E))\xrightarrow{i_*}K_\bullet(\mathbb P(F))\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(i\) denotes the closed immersion \(\mathbb P(E)\longrightarrow\mathbb P(F)\) defined by \(u\). By 3.2, we have \(K_\bullet(\mathbb P(E))=K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(\mathbb P(E))\), \(K_\bullet(\mathbb P(F))=K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(\mathbb P(F))\). Since \(\mathbb P(E)\) and \(\mathbb P(F)\) are smooth, \(i\) is a regular immersion, and we therefore have a homomorphism \(i_*:K'(\mathbb P(E))\longrightarrow K'(\mathbb P(F))\); the homomorphism between the \(K_\bullet\) is then obtained by tensoring the homomorphism between the \(K'\) by \(K_\bullet(S)\); to see this it suffices to show that for \(a\in K_\bullet(S)\) and
\(b\in K'(\mathbb P(E))\), we have
\[i_*(b.h^*(a))=i_*(b).g^*(a),\]
where \(g\) and \(h\) are the structural morphisms of \(\mathbb P(F)\) and \(\mathbb P(E)\). Since \(h=g\circ i\), we have:
\[i_*(b.h^*(a))=i_*(b.i^*g^*(a))=g^*(a).i_*(b)\quad\text{d'après IV 2.12.4.}\]
By (3.4.1), we therefore have:
\[K_\bullet(P)\simeq K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}\bigl(K'(\mathbb P(F))/\operatorname{Im}K'(\mathbb P(E))\bigr).\]

Let \(\xi_F\) and \(\xi_E\) be the classes of the canonical invertible sheaves on \(\mathbb P(F)\) and \(\mathbb P(E)\). Then, by VI 1.1, \(K'(\mathbb P(F))\) (resp. \(K'(\mathbb P(E))\)) admits as a basis over \(K'(S)\) the elements \(\xi_F^k\) for \(k=0,\ldots,-n\) (resp. \(\xi_E^k\) for \(k=0,\ldots,-n+1\)). The projection formula gives:
\[i_*(\xi_E^k)=i_*(i^*(\xi_F^k))=\xi_F^k i_*(1).\]

If \(I\) is the ideal of \(\mathbb P(E)\) in \(\mathbb P(F)\), \(I\) is an invertible ideal, and we have \(i_*(1)=1-I\). Moreover, \(I\) is defined by the ideal \(J\) of \(S(F)\), the kernel of \(S(F)\longrightarrow S(E)\), which is generated by a sub-Module of \(F\) isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_S\); consequently, \(I\simeq\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(F)}(-1)\). The image of \(K'(\mathbb P(E))\) is therefore the sub-\(K'(S)\)-module generated by the \(\xi_F^k-\xi_F^{k-1}\) for \(k=0,\ldots,-n+1\). The homomorphism \(K'(S)\longrightarrow K'(\mathbb P(F))/\operatorname{Im}(K'(\mathbb P(E)))\) is therefore an isomorphism, and consequently the same holds for the homomorphism \(K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\).
4. Principal bundles under the groups \(Gl(n)_S\)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(G\) the \(S\)-group \(Gl(n)_S\), \(P\) a torsor under \(G\), and \(E\) the locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(n\) corresponding to \(P\). We denote by \(E\) the class of \(E\) in \(K'(S)\), and by \(J\) the \(\lambda\)-ideal generated
by \((E-n)\). If \(f\) is the structural morphism of \(P\), then \(f^*\), which is defined since \(f\) is flat, is surjective and defines an isomorphism:
\[K_\bullet(S)/J.K_\bullet(S)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K_\bullet(P).\]

Let \(E_0=(\mathcal O_S)^n\), \(e_1,\ldots,e_n\) the obvious basis of \(E_0\), and \(E_0^{(i)}\) the sub-Module of \(E_0\) generated by \(e_1,\ldots,e_i\). The subfunctor of \(G\) which to every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\) associates the subgroup of automorphisms of \(E_{0S'}\) which leave invariant the submodules \(E_{0S'}^{(i)}\) for every \(i\) is representable by a subgroup scheme \(B\) of \(G\), which is a Borel subgroup of \(G\). The quotient sheaf \(G/B\) (for the fpqc topology) is representable by the flag scheme of \(E_0\), which will be denoted \(X_0\). Let \(L_0^{(i)}\) be the canonical quotients of the inverse image of \(E_0\) on \(X_0\), \(G_0^{(i)}\) the kernel of \(E_{0X_0}\longrightarrow L_0^{(i)}\), and \(F_0^{(i)}=G_0^{(i)}/G_0^{(i-1)}\simeq\ker(L_0^{(i)}\longrightarrow L_0^{(i-1)})\).

Moreover, \(B\) is a semi-direct product of its unipotent radical \(U\) and its maximal subtorus \(T\) leaving the \(e_i\) invariant. Let \(Y_0=G/U\). Then \(Y_0\) is a torsor with base \(X_0\) under the group \(T_{X_0}\). If \(S'\) is a \(S\)-scheme, the datum of a \(S'\)-point of \(Y_0\) is equivalent to the datum, over a \(S''\) faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S'\), of an automorphism of \(E_{0S''}\) modulo the unipotents of \(U_{S''}\), that is to say of a flag of \(E_{0S''}\), the submodules of the flag being free, and for every \(i\), of a class of elements \(f_i^\alpha\) free over \(\mathcal O_{S''}\) such that \(f_i^\alpha-f_i^\beta\in G_{0S''}^{(i+1)}\), or again to the datum of a flag of \(E_{0S''}\) and for every \(i\) of a basis of \(F_{0S''}^{(i)}\), this datum being subject to the glueing condition on \(S''\times_{S'}S''\); by fpqc descent, this amounts to the same datum over \(S'\). We then see immediately that \(Y_0\) is the affine \(X_0\)-scheme defined by the algebra:
\[A_0=\bigoplus_{k_i\in\mathbb Z}F_0^{(1)\otimes k_1}\otimes F_0^{(2)\otimes k_2}\otimes\cdots\otimes F_0^{(n)\otimes k_n}.\]

The group \(G\) acts on \(E_0\), on \(X_0\) endowed with the sheaves \(L_0^{(i)}\) and on \(Y_0\). If we take the product of these objects with \(P\), under \(G\), we obtain the sheaf \(E\) associated to \(P\), the scheme \(X=\mathbb D(E)=P/B\) of flags of \(E\), and the scheme \(Y=P/U\). We shall denote the canonical sheaves on \(X\) by the same letters as their counterparts on \(X_0\). We therefore have the diagram:
\[\begin{array}{ccccc}&&Y=P/U&&\\&\nearrow&&\searrow&\\P&&&&X=P/B=\mathbb D(E)\\&\searrow&&\swarrow&\\&&S&&\end{array}\]
We remark that \(P\) is a torsor with base \(Y\) and structural group \(U_Y\), and that \(Y\) is a torsor with base \(X\) and structural group \(T_X\), defined by the \(\mathcal O_X\)-Algebra:
\[A=\bigoplus_{k_i\in\mathbb Z}F^{(1)\otimes k_1}\otimes\cdots\otimes F^{(n)\otimes k_n}.\]
The homomorphism \(K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P)\) therefore factors as:
\[K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(Y)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(P).\]

By 3.1, we have \(K_\bullet(X)=K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}K'(X)\). Let \(\xi_i\) be the class of \(F^{(i)}\) in \(K'(X)\). Then by 2.2, \(K_\bullet(Y)\) is the quotient of \(K_\bullet(X)\) by the submodule generated by the \((\xi_i-1)K_\bullet(X)\). If \(I\) is the ideal of \(K'(X)\) generated by the \(\xi_i-1\), we therefore have:
\[K_\bullet(Y)=K_\bullet(S)\otimes_{K'(S)}(K'(X)/I).\]
Now by VI 4.8, \(K'(X)\) is the \(K'(S)\)-algebra generated by the \(\xi_i-1\), subject to the relations deduced from:
\[\gamma_t(E-n)=\prod_{i=1}^n\gamma_t(\xi_i-1).\]
Hence \(K'(X)/I=K'(S)/J\), where \(J\) is the ideal generated by the \(\gamma^j(E-n)\) for \(j=1,\ldots,n\), hence the \(\lambda\)-ideal generated by \(E-n\). Consequently,
\[K_\bullet(Y)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K_\bullet(S)/JK_\bullet(S).\]
We complete the proof by the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(B\) a parabolic subgroup of a reductive \(S\)-group, and \(P\) a torsor under the unipotent radical \(U\) of \(B\); then we have an isomorphism:

\[K_\bullet(S)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K_\bullet(P).\]
Indeed, there exists a sequence of subgroup schemes of \(U\):
\[U_0=U\supset U_1\supset U_2\supset\cdots\supset U_p=e,\]

closed in \(B\), finite since \(B\) is noetherian, and such that, for every \(i\), \(U_i/U_{i+1}\) is isomorphic to a vector \(S\)-group (SGA 3 XXVI 2.1). Consequently, \(P\longrightarrow S\) factors through a sequence of torsors under vector groups, whence the result by 3.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(G=Gl(n)_S\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(K_\bullet(S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(G)\) is an isomorphism.
Follows immediately from 4.1, applied to \(P=G\), since then \(E=\mathcal O_S^n\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4.

Under the hypotheses of 4.1, suppose \(S\) regular. Then we have an isomorphism:

\[K'(S)/J\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K'(P).\]

If \(S\) is regular, \(G\) is also regular, and \(P\) as well. We therefore have \(K'(S)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K_\bullet(S)\), \(K'(P)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K_\bullet(P)\), whence the result by 4.1.
